notes: Low is inferred from Britannica's approximate 3,000 total military killed with the great bulk Chinese, minus the commonly cited 526 foreign soldiers killed. High reflects secondary summaries stating about 20,000 Imperial troops probably died. No audited Qing/Boxer military death roll is available.

The numeric low includes only Chinese Christian and foreign-civilian categories that recur in multiple summaries. It excludes the unknown number of non-Christian Chinese civilians killed by Boxer/Qing forces.

Available Preston/Clark excerpts state that over 400 Chinese and foreigners died at the Peitang, including 166 children, but the figure mixes direct mine/explosion deaths, siege deprivation, and possibly other causes; it is not safely separable from direct-civilian categories.

A commonly cited compact figure is 526 foreign soldiers killed. U.S. official histories give component figures such as 95 American casualties at Tientsin and 7 U.S. Legation Guard killed; the 526 figure should be treated as a secondary-summary number, not a fully audited all-cause service-death regi
The numeric low includes only the Amur/Blagoveshchensk massacres. The broader Allied civilian toll is certainly higher but not quantifiable with the sources consulted.

Allied looting, forced requisitions, village destruction, and expulsions caused displacement and likely secondary deaths, but no defensible separate death count is available. The Sixty-Four Villages East of the River lost Chinese residency rights after the Amur massacres.
Klein notes looting and forcible seizure of provisions and says Allied strategy and tactics took a great death toll among innocent Chinese, but the indirect share is not quantifiable.

Counts primarily U.S. military deaths from all causes. Sources give 4,165, over 4,200, or about 4,300 American military deaths; local Filipino auxiliaries are not cleanly included because sources often combine killed and wounded.

Direct civilian killing is the least cleanly separated category. The 34,000 figure is used as a conservative point estimate for direct-war Filipino civilian deaths, but some deaths in combat zones may blur civilian/combatant categories.

Official Bureau of Health records reported 109,461 cholera deaths in 1902-1904; war summaries often cite up to 200,000 noncombatant deaths from cholera/disease in the war's immediate aftermath. Attribution to U.S. action is partial: wartime disruption, displacement, reconcentration, and food destruc

Bell's reconcentration policy and destruction of food supplies increased civilian mortality and disease transmission. Some modern summaries attribute over 11,000 deaths directly to reconcentration, but overlap with cholera and broader disease/famine deaths prevents a clean additive estimate.

side: First Philippine Republic / Philippine Republican Army
notes: U.S. Army Combat Studies Institute gives 16,000-20,000 insurrectos killed; U.S. State Department gives over 20,000 Filipino combatants dead.

side: First Philippine Republic / Philippine Republican Army
notes: Filipino guerrillas assassinated collaborators and attacked U.S. soldiers, but no credible aggregate civilian-death estimate for civilians directly killed by Philippine Republican forces was found. The Balangiga attack killed U.S. soldiers, not civilians.

Common summaries give 34,000-52,623 Russian combatants killed or died of wounds and 9,300-18,830 died of disease, for 43,300-71,453 total dead. OnWar gives 71,453 Russian fatalities, citing Clodfelter, COW85, Encyclopaedia Britannica, and Kohn. NLM cross-checks with 40,000-70,000 Russian deaths.

The only specific non-Chinese direct civilian deaths found were the two British fishermen killed at Dogger Bank. The much larger Chinese civilian toll is not cleanly attributable by belligerent, although Russian violence against Chinese civilians is specifically reported.

Common summaries give 47,152-47,400 killed, 11,424-11,500 died of wounds, and 21,802-27,200 died of disease, for 80,378-99,000 Japanese dead. NLM gives a cross-check point estimate of 85,600 Japanese deaths. The 1908 British War Office report reproduces Japanese medical tables with 43,219 killed in
No responsible source found separates Chinese civilian deaths into Japanese-caused versus Russian-caused categories. The high value is an attribution upper bound only, not a point estimate.

Explicit breakdown found: 676 killed in action, 76 missing, and 689 died of disease, totaling 1,441 military dead or missing. A separate aggregate says 1,749 Germans died in the conflict; subtracting the separately listed 100 civilians gives an implied upper military figure of 1,649, but the 1,441 b

Direct civilian deaths caused by German forces are certainly part of the genocide, but the literature generally reports aggregate Herero/Nama population losses rather than a clean direct-killing civilian subtotal.

Aggregate Herero deaths from the German campaign, including direct killing, desert expulsion, blocked return to water and land, disease, starvation, camps, and forced labour. Madley gives 40,000-70,000 Herero killed; other summaries give 40,000-80,000 or a point estimate around 65,000.

Aggregate Nama deaths from the German campaign, usually presented as about half of a prewar population of about 20,000. This includes direct killing and indirect deaths from camps, forced labour, starvation, disease, and displacement.

A major killing mechanism after Waterberg/Ohamakari. Madley reports that about 1,000 Herero reached British Bechuanaland while the vast majority died of thirst and hunger en route, but no precise standalone desert-death total is available.

Madley cites official German figures of about 7,700 deaths among 17,200 Herero and Nama internees, about 45%. A secondary military study citing Sarkin reports 7,682 of 15,000 Herero and 2,000 Nama died from 1904 to 1908; because the wording is ambiguous, this is treated as a possible high rather tha

An official report recorded 1,032 deaths among 1,795 prisoners in eight months. Another account says about 3,500 Herero and Nama were sent to Shark Island and only 193 returned; this implies up to 3,307 deaths or non-returns, but the figures are not strictly equivalent.

Herero and Nama combatant deaths cannot be separated reliably from civilian deaths. Sources describe about 8,000-10,000 Herero warriors early in the war and about 3,000-5,000 Herero combatants defeated at Waterberg/Ohamakari, but do not provide a reliable killed-in-action subtotal distinct from the
German civilian deaths caused by Herero/Nama forces were concentrated in the initial uprising. They are counted separately from German military deaths.

No reliable side-specific national total found. The all-faction combat-death range used in this report is 125,000 to 250,000. Veracruz produced about 193 Mexican soldier deaths, but that is only one event and not a Federal/Huertista war total.
Direct civilian killing by federal/Huertista forces is documented qualitatively but not countable nationally from the sources found.
Federal campaigns contributed to indirect mortality, but McCaa's national excess-death estimate is not apportioned by faction.
No side-specific national total found. Early-phase Maderista losses are included in aggregate Revolution combat-death estimates.
This is a documented event floor, not a national total for all Maderista direct civilian killings.
No reliable national total by side found. Event reports include 138 Constitutionalist killed in one Celaya phase, but this is not a total.
Executions and reprisals are documented, but no reliable direct-civilian national total by Constitutionalist/Carrancista forces was found.
Constitutionalist campaigns contributed to the national demographic disaster, but indirect deaths are not apportioned by faction in the demographic literature found.
No reliable national total by side found. Event-level reports include about 4,000 Villistas dead in one Celaya phase and about 70 to 100 attackers killed during the Columbus raid.
This is an event floor for two prominent incidents, not a national total of all Villista direct civilian killings.

side: Zapatistas / Liberation Army of the South
notes: No reliable Zapatista military-death total found; losses are included in aggregate Revolution combat-death estimates.

side: Zapatistas / Liberation Army of the South
notes: Direct killings are documented qualitatively but not countable nationally from the sources found.

side: United States forces, Texas Rangers, border law-enforcement, and vigilantes
notes: Approximate event-based range from Veracruz, Columbus, and other Punitive Expedition/border incidents; not a single official all-cause total from the sources found. Veracruz caused about 19-21 U.S. servicemen killed; Columbus caused 8 U.S. soldiers killed in AP's account and 14 military American cas

side: United States forces, Texas Rangers, border law-enforcement, and vigilantes
notes: Porvenir is the confirmed point estimate. Broader Texas border violence is relevant context but overlaps only partly with the Mexican Revolution as a war.

side: Unattributed aggregate of all Mexican belligerents and war conditions
notes: COW-derived strict battle deaths are 125,000. Other summarized estimates give about 200,000 to 250,000 combat or military deaths.

side: Unattributed aggregate of all Mexican belligerents and war conditions
notes: This is an aggregate literature-derived estimate, not a per-side attribution.

side: Unattributed aggregate of all Mexican belligerents and war conditions
notes: Residual estimate. McCaa reports responsible mortality scenarios from about 0.5 million to 2.0 million excess deaths and prefers 1.4 million excess deaths. Subtracting broad combat/direct-death components produces a residual indirect-death range; this is an accounting inference, not a published fact

side: Unattributed aggregate of all Mexican belligerents and war conditions
notes: Modern scholarship gives 300,000 to 600,000 flu deaths in Mexico. McCaa treats the flu as part of the broader revolutionary demographic disaster because war reduced public-health capacity.

About 65,000 Bulgarian dead across both Balkan Wars is the most useful combined-war estimate. The lower value reflects widely cited First Balkan War non-recoverable deaths, often broken down as 14,000 killed and 19,000 dead of disease.
No reliable aggregate civilian-death total caused by Bulgarian forces was found. The low value is only a documented-event minimum for Doxato and Serres, not a total.

Bulgaria contributed to the wider Ottoman Muslim refugee and mortality catastrophe, but the available sources do not cleanly separate Bulgarian-caused deaths from deaths caused by Serbian, Greek, Montenegrin, irregular, disease, hunger, and flight conditions.
Hall gives at least 36,000 Serbian dead; another Hall book snippet gives 36,550 dead and 55,000 wounded across both wars.
Serbian responsibility is central in accounts of Albanian massacres, but high estimates usually combine Serbian and Montenegrin forces and may include deaths from flight, hunger, or cold.
Displacement estimates such as 60,000-300,000 expelled are cited in secondary summaries, but deaths among the displaced are not separated from massacre totals.
Hall gives about 9,500 Greek dead across the wars. A First Balkan War breakdown gives 5,169 killed and 1,550 dead of disease, or 6,719 deaths before Second Balkan War losses.
No reliable aggregate civilian-death total caused by Greek forces was found. The low value is a documented-event minimum, not a total.
Greek village-burning and forced flight are documented in Carnegie materials, but deaths from displacement, exposure, or disease are not aggregated by perpetrator.
Hall gives about 3,000 Montenegrin dead. A First Balkan War breakdown gives 2,836 killed and 406 dead of disease.
No reliable Montenegro-only civilian death total found.
Montenegrin contribution to displacement is documented but not numerically separable from Serbian operations.
Romania's Second Balkan War deaths were overwhelmingly from cholera rather than combat; estimates are about 1,500-1,600.
No reliable evidence found of a significant documented civilian death toll directly caused by Romanian forces in the brief 1913 invasion of Bulgaria.
Hall gives Ottoman military dead as many as 125,000. The figure should be treated as a maximum-style point estimate rather than a precise count.
The direct-death minimum is based on Carnegie's summarized figure; the high is unknown because larger figures usually combine deaths and expulsions.
Ottoman reconquest of Eastern Thrace caused mass killing, burning, and expulsion. The often-cited 200,000 figure is killed-or-expelled, not deaths alone.

Range from Prost's table in 1914-1918 Online: Urlanis low and Prost evaluation high. Britannica/U.S. War Department cross-check gives 5,142,631 Allied killed and died. Definitions vary on disease, POWs, missing, and colonial laborers.
No reliable all-theater total for civilians directly killed by Allied forces was found. The numeric low is only a documented partial figure for Allied air raids on Germany.

Civilian excess deaths associated with food and fuel shortages under the Allied blockade of Germany. Attribution is contested because German administration, black markets, influenza, and post-armistice blockade continuation also mattered.

Co-caused by Allied naval blockade, Ottoman requisitioning and administrative failure, transport disruption, and natural disasters. Not additive with the same event listed under Central Powers responsibility.

Ottoman records cited by 1914-1918 Online list 701,166 Muslim refugees dying on the road; total Muslim deaths in Eastern Anatolia are estimated around 1.5 million. Deaths mix Russian invasion, Armenian bands, starvation, disease, massacre, and displacement; attribution is not clean.

Minimum from British, Belgian, and Portuguese colonial laborer deaths in 1914-1918 Online; French laborer deaths are listed as unknown, so this is an undercount. Some laborers were military auxiliaries rather than civilians.

Approximate African civilian losses from war zones, rebellions, famine, malnutrition, and disease. Allied colonial powers caused much of the mobilization and requisitioning, but German and other forces also contributed; not additive with Central Powers African responsibility.

Range from Prost's table in 1914-1918 Online: Overmans low and Prost evaluation high. Britannica/U.S. War Department cross-check gives a lower 3,386,200 killed and died, less inclusive for some categories.

Direct civilian-killing totals are incomplete. Genocidal deportations and famine policies are recorded under deaths_from_actions to avoid double counting direct massacre and indirect death-march/starvation deaths.
Range combines USHMM low estimate and broader 1914-1918 Online summary. Deaths resulted from Ottoman deportation, massacre, starvation, disease, death marches, and forced labor.
Britannica states estimates vary but upwards of 250,000 Assyrians were killed during World War I.
Academic estimates for the broader 1914-1922/23 period. The WWI-only portion cannot be cleanly separated from post-armistice violence.
Co-caused by Ottoman requisitioning and administration, Allied naval blockade, transport breakdown, and natural disasters. Not additive with the same event listed under Allied responsibility.

Civilian losses from Serbian war-loss estimates, mostly typhus, famine, disease, occupation, and clashes. Caused by Central Powers invasion/occupation conditions but not all deaths were deliberate killings.
Civilian losses in Romania from 1914-1918 Online estimates; causes include disease, hunger, displacement, and occupation conditions.
Belgian civilians who died during expulsion, deportation, imprisonment, or death sentences under German occupation; separate from the approximately 6,000-6,500 murdered in 1914.
1914-1918 Online table assigns about 14,000 African soldier deaths and 6,400 African laborer deaths to German colonies.

East African civilian losses from war zone, rebellion, and famine. German operations and requisitioning contributed, but Allied requisitioning and campaigning also contributed; not solely attributable to Germany and not additive with Allied African responsibility.

war_name: Armenian Genocide and late Ottoman genocides of Armenians, Assyrians, and Greeks
side: Ottoman Empire / Committee of Union and Progress / Turkish National Movement
notes: No reliable casualty count exists for Ottoman/Turkish forces killed specifically in genocide operations. Ottoman World War I all-front military dead/missing are often given as 771844, but that figure is not rolled into the genocide total.

side: Ottoman Empire / Committee of Union and Progress / Turkish National Movement
notes: Direct civilian killings by Ottoman/CUP/Turkish Nationalist forces were extensive, but a clean all-victim direct-killing subtotal is not available.

war_name: Armenian Genocide and late Ottoman genocides of Armenians, Assyrians, and Greeks

notes: Aggregate Armenian genocide deaths. USHMM gives 664000-1200000 for the main 1915-1916 genocide; broader historical ranges commonly extend to 1500000 for 1915-1923. Direct and indirect components are not cleanly separable.

war_name: Armenian Genocide and late Ottoman genocides of Armenians, Assyrians, and Greeks
side: Ottoman Empire / Committee of Union and Progress / Turkish National Movement
notes: Tatoyan reports that detailed contemporary estimates assigned about 400000 Armenian deaths in 1916 to concentration camps in Syria and Mesopotamia due to starvation, disease, and subsequent actions. This is a subcomponent of the Armenian aggregate, not an additional total.

war_name: Armenian Genocide and late Ottoman genocides of Armenians, Assyrians, and Greeks
side: Ottoman Empire / Committee of Union and Progress / Turkish National Movement
notes: Britannica says upwards of 250000 Assyrians were killed during World War I; 250000-300000 is a common range in Assyrian and secondary summaries.

war_name: Armenian Genocide and late Ottoman genocides of Armenians, Assyrians, and Greeks
side: Ottoman Empire / Committee of Union and Progress / Turkish National Movement
notes: Academic estimates for Ottoman Greek deaths in Anatolia during 1914-1922/1923 range roughly 289000-750000. Higher claims exist but are not used in the main roll-up.

war_name: Armenian Genocide and late Ottoman genocides of Armenians, Assyrians, and Greeks
side: Ottoman Empire / Committee of Union and Progress / Turkish National Movement
notes: Some sources state that at least 200000 people in Armenia, mostly refugees, died from starvation or disease in 1918-1920 partly due to Turkish blockade and crop destruction. This is flagged separately and not added to the main roll-up because of possible overlap with Armenian survivor/refugee counts

war_name: Armenian Genocide and late Ottoman genocides of Armenians, Assyrians, and Greeks

notes: No reliable military death figure was found that is specific to this genocide dataset rather than the wider Caucasus front and Armenian-Turkish wars.

war_name: Armenian Genocide and late Ottoman genocides of Armenians, Assyrians, and Greeks
side: Russian Empire / Armenian volunteer and Armenian National forces
notes: Armenian/Russian-associated forces committed revenge killings and massacres of Muslim civilians, but reliable aggregate numbers are lacking and politically contested.

war_name: Armenian Genocide and late Ottoman genocides of Armenians, Assyrians, and Greeks

notes: Greek military deaths in 1919-1922 belong to the wider Greco-Turkish War and are not counted as part of the Ottoman genocides dataset.

war_name: Armenian Genocide and late Ottoman genocides of Armenians, Assyrians, and Greeks
side: Kingdom of Greece / Greek occupation forces and associated bands
notes: Greek occupation forces and associated bands committed serious atrocities against Turkish/Muslim civilians, but no reliable all-region aggregate death toll was found.

war_name: Russian Civil War (including Red/White Terror, famines, and anti-Jewish pogroms)
notes: Low combines Urlanis's 125,000 Red Army KIA with Patterson's cited Soviet-demographic estimate of about 100,000 Red Army typhus deaths in 1918–1920. High is the broader Red Army dead-or-missing figure reported in military-loss summaries and is not directly comparable to KIA-only estimates.

war_name: Russian Civil War (including Red/White Terror, famines, and anti-Jewish pogroms)
notes: The total adds conservative Red Terror estimates to the Suny-derived Red pogrom estimate. This avoids using very high broad 'Red Terror' estimates that may include indirect deaths or overlap with famine and pacification campaigns.

war_name: Russian Civil War (including Red/White Terror, famines, and anti-Jewish pogroms)
notes: Total 1921–1922 famine mortality, not a Bolshevik-only toll. Soviet requisitioning and War Communism are identified as contributing factors alongside drought, prior war destruction, transport collapse, livestock loss, epidemics, and Allied blockade.

war_name: Russian Civil War (including Red/White Terror, famines, and anti-Jewish pogroms)
notes: Estimates vary by definition. Some summaries cite about 15,000 killed during suppression; Sennikov's broader estimate gives about 240,000 total Tambov regional losses from war, executions, and imprisonment in concentration camps in 1920–1922. Not added mechanically to Red Terror or famine totals bec

war_name: Russian Civil War (including Red/White Terror, famines, and anti-Jewish pogroms)
notes: Decossackization estimates are highly contentious and often mix deaths with deportations; this range should be read as killed-or-deported rather than confirmed deaths alone.

war_name: Russian Civil War (including Red/White Terror, famines, and anti-Jewish pogroms)
notes: Low is Urlanis's KIA estimate for White armies and Poles combined, so it is not cleanly White-only. High is the broad 650,000 White Army killed figure reported in secondary military-loss summaries.

war_name: Russian Civil War (including Red/White Terror, famines, and anti-Jewish pogroms)
notes: The structured range combines White Terror estimates excluding pogroms with the Suny-derived White pogrom share. White Terror figures are among the most contested numbers in the conflict.

war_name: Russian Civil War (including Red/White Terror, famines, and anti-Jewish pogroms)
notes: White armies and White-controlled regimes contributed to displacement, transport disruption, requisitions, imprisonment, and epidemic conditions, but no reliable White-only indirect-death toll was found. Direct terror and pogrom deaths are counted above.

war_name: Russian Civil War (including Red/White Terror, famines, and anti-Jewish pogroms)
side: Ukrainian nationalist, Green, peasant-insurgent, and local warlord forces
notes: No reliable aggregate military-death estimate separated these heterogeneous forces from Red, White, Polish, and civilian losses.

war_name: Russian Civil War (including Red/White Terror, famines, and anti-Jewish pogroms)
side: Ukrainian nationalist, Green, peasant-insurgent, and local warlord forces
notes: This side's most clearly attributable mass-casualty category is anti-Jewish pogrom killing. It excludes non-Jewish civilian killings for which no aggregate separated estimate was found.

war_name: Russian Civil War (including Red/White Terror, famines, and anti-Jewish pogroms)
notes: No reliable aggregate military-death estimate was found for all foreign and border-state intervention forces. Urlanis's 175,500 KIA estimate combines White armies and Poles and cannot be used as a separate foreign total.

war_name: Russian Civil War (including Red/White Terror, famines, and anti-Jewish pogroms)
notes: Foreign and border-state forces committed local violence, but I found no responsible aggregate direct-civilian death estimate separated from other belligerents.

war_name: Russian Civil War (including Red/White Terror, famines, and anti-Jewish pogroms)
notes: The Allied economic blockade is identified as a contributing factor to the 1921–1922 famine, whose total mortality is commonly cited around 5 million and ranged in estimates from 1–10 million. No blockade-only death toll was found.

side: Kingdom of Greece / Greek occupation forces
notes: Low/point adds official Greek categories 19,362 killed plus 4,878 died outside combat. High adds 18,095 missing, but the missing category may overlap with prisoners and presumed captivity deaths. About 7,000 Greek prisoners are often presumed to have died in Turkish captivity, but this is not added

side: Kingdom of Greece / Greek occupation forces
notes: At least 15,000 direct civilian killings are supported as a minimum by Rummel. Higher direct-only totals cannot be separated reliably from refugee deaths, disease, starvation, and population loss.

side: Kingdom of Greece / Greek occupation forces
notes: Justin McCarthy estimates 640,000 Muslim deaths in the Greek/British zones in 1919–1922, with many deaths among refugees, but Travlos/Erickson describe the apportionment as arbitrary. This is a contested population-loss estimate, not a direct count.

side: Kingdom of Greece / Greek occupation forces
notes: Sources report large-scale destruction, including 30,000+ buildings and 250+ villages burned in one summary and 160,739 destroyed buildings in McCarthy's discussion of Lausanne-era Turkish statistics. Mortality attributable to this destruction cannot be separated cleanly.

Low/point adds 9,167 killed and 2,474 died of wounds or non-combat causes. High adds 11,150 missing, but this may overcount because missing and prisoner categories are uncertain. OnWar gives 20,000 Turkish fatalities for the narrower 1921–1922 interstate-war coding.
The direct-killing high is approximate and deliberately not added mechanically event by event because some deaths are included in broader Greek genocide and Smyrna catastrophe estimates.

Rummel estimates 264,000 Greek civilians killed by Turkish nationalist forces during 1919–1922. The 750,000 high belongs to the wider academic range for Ottoman Greek deaths across the 1914–1922/23 period and therefore overextends the specific war period.
Samsun deportations are summarized as 21,000 deported and about 10,000 dead; ICRC article states that violence in Pontus killed an estimated 11,000 people. These may overlap.

The 1923 Lausanne Convention made the exchange compulsory from 1 May 1923. ICRC summarizes the exchange as involving roughly 1.5 million people. This is displacement rather than a death toll, but it was the institutional endpoint of wartime ethnic cleansing.

Low is the official Spanish death toll for Annual and the subsequent rout to Melilla, including Moroccan colonial troops serving Spain. High uses the COW state-side battle-death figure as an upper-bound indicator, but this may include or overlap French state-side losses and is not a clean Spanish-on
Civilian deaths caused directly by Spanish forces were not systematically counted; available sources emphasize that civilian deaths were likely underestimated or omitted.
Spanish policy included blockades and destruction of resources, but starvation/disease deaths are unquantified.
Local and NGO claims link later cancer and malformations to mustard-gas exposure, but no independent international scientific study provides a reliable death toll.
Operations affected livestock, markets, agricultural land, dwellings, and harvests; indirect deaths are not quantified.

The 8,628 figure is a commonly cited final French deaths-from-battle-and-disease total. A French-language summary gives 10,000 French dead, while the ICRC review reports that some sources suggest 18,000 French troops died but warns that these figures should be treated with caution.
French direct civilian killing is well supported qualitatively but not numerically.
French participation in blockade and counter-insurgency pressure likely produced indirect deaths, but no credible numerical estimate was found.
Military operations affected food production infrastructure, but deaths from starvation, disease, or displacement cannot be quantified from available sources.
French chemical warfare in 1925 is documented, but no reliable death toll is available.

side: Republic of the Rif / Riffian tribal forces
notes: Low is the nearly 10,000 Riffian dead reported in a review of Schiavon's study. COW gives 40,000 non-state battle-related combatant fatalities. The ICRC review cites some sources giving 30,000 Riffian troop deaths but warns that such figures are uncertain.

side: Republic of the Rif / Riffian tribal forces
notes: No responsible estimate was found for civilians directly killed by Riffian forces. The better documented Riffian atrocities involved captured Spanish soldiers and prisoners of war rather than civilians.

side: Republic of the Rif / Riffian tribal forces
notes: In one documented prisoner group, 550 Spanish prisoners were repatriated in 1923 and only 326 returned; the remainder died of hunger or were executed. This overlaps with Spanish military deaths and should not be added to the grand total.

side: Republic of the Rif / Riffian tribal forces
notes: The ICRC review reports that on several occasions, including Annual, Mont Aroui/Monte Arruit, and Nador, Spanish troops were executed and mutilated, with deaths estimated at 10,000-14,000. This likely overlaps heavily with the official Annual/rout military death toll.

war_name: Irish War of Independence and Civil War
notes: Low combines 491 IRA Volunteers killed in O'Halpin/Ó Corráin-derived War of Independence figures and 438 anti-Treaty fatalities in the UCC Civil War project. High allows for other summaries giving about 550 IRA dead in the War of Independence and roughly 500 anti-Treaty IRA dead in older Civil War e

war_name: Irish War of Independence and Civil War
notes: The low value is a documented minimum: 281 IRA-attributed non-combatant deaths to 1921 plus at least 19 clearly documented 1922 civilian massacre victims. The national high is unknown because Civil War and Belfast/partition civilian deaths are not fully attributed in accessible aggregate sources.

side: Pro-Treaty Irish Free State / National Army side
notes: Pro-Treaty forces directly caused some civilian deaths, especially through fighting in Dublin, checkpoint shootings, and security operations, but a defensible national perpetrator total is not available in the public aggregate sources consulted.

All-cause military deaths while serving. Responsible sources give more than 50,000 Bolivian deaths, 50,000, 52,397, about 60,000, and up to 65,000. Causes include combat, wounds, disease, dehydration, exposure, snakebite, and deaths in captivity. POW deaths in Paraguayan captivity are treated as a s

No reliable aggregate estimate found for civilians directly killed by Bolivian forces. The war zone was sparsely settled but not empty; Indigenous and civilian disruption is documented, but side-attributed direct civilian death figures are not responsibly quantifiable from the sources consulted.

All-cause military deaths while serving. Common estimates include 30,000 Paraguayan dead, 31,500 dead and missing, about 36,000, and 40,000. Causes include combat, wounds, disease, dehydration, exposure, and other service-related causes.

No reliable aggregate estimate found for civilians directly killed by Paraguayan forces. Sources document civilian capture, Indigenous disruption, epidemics, and forced migration, but not a clean direct-civilian-killing total by Paraguayan forces.

Captain Dent Sharp / Chaco Peace Conference materials listed 1,097 Bolivian POWs as having died in captivity; the author of Politics of the Chaco Peace Conference reasoned that perhaps about 3,000 Bolivians died in Paraguayan detention camps. These are military POW deaths and are treated as part of

Bolivian POW labor in Paraguay is documented by ICRC archival material and by scholarship on Bolivian prisoners, but deaths specifically attributable to forced labor, as distinct from captivity, deprivation, illness, accidents, wounds, and suicide, are not separately quantified.

Low is the Italian estimate of 50,000 killed on the northern front plus 20,000 on the southern front. High combines the Ethiopian government's postwar categories: 275,000 soldiers killed in action, 78,500 Patriots killed in hostilities during the occupation, and 24,000 Patriots killed by summary cou

No evidence found for large-scale Ethiopian direct killing of Ethiopian civilians. The main identified civilian victims of Ethiopian action were Italian civilians/workers, especially at Gondrand; the larger upper bound is uncertain.

side: Kingdom of Italy / Italian East African forces

High uses Del Boca's esti

side: Kingdom of Italy / Italian East African forces
notes: Direct civilian death totals are assembled from major documented categories. They should not be added mechanically to the Ethiopian government's 760,300 total without checking for overlap, especially for 1937 reprisals.

side: Kingdom of Italy / Italian East African forces
notes: Ethiopian government postwar claim for persons who died in concentration camps from privation and maltreatment. The existence of camps is well attested, but the 35,000 mortality figure is less independently reconstructed than the major massacres.

notes: Ethiopian government postwar claim for deaths from privation after village destruction. Related claims include destruction of 525,000 houses, 2,000 churches, and mass confiscation or slaughter of livestock. This is the largest and most uncertain category.

Common tabulations give about 110,000 Republican combatants killed, while Cambridge's lower combined military total implies a lower floor if totals are scaled down. Includes foreign volunteers serving with Republican forces; International Brigade deaths alone are commonly estimated around 3,600-6,50
Republican direct civilian killings were concentrated in the first months of the war amid state collapse and revolutionary violence; they declined as Republican authorities restored control.
Common tabulations give about 90,000 Nationalist combatants killed. Foreign losses included roughly 3,300 Italian CTV deaths and about 298 Condor Legion deaths in specialist tabulations.

The point estimate uses about 100,000 wartime executions plus about 11,000-12,000 bombing/shelling deaths. The high end risks including some immediate postwar Francoist repression, which many sources mix with wartime counts.

Cambridge notes thousands of deaths beyond the normal rate from stress, disease, and malnutrition, and broader summaries discuss wide excess-mortality ranges. Not rolled into the 1936-1939 total. USHMM says that by 1940 more than 500,000 individuals were rounded up and sent to about 60 concentration camps, with many used for forced labor or tried by military courts, but it does not provide a single death count for this category.

Low uses Westad/Mitter-style combat-death estimate; high uses National WWII Museum's 3-4 million Chinese military deaths. The official ROC/Nationalist-only figure of about 1.32 million killed plus 120,000 missing is narrower and excludes many categories.

Direct civilian deaths caused by Chinese forces are much less systematically counted than Japanese atrocities. The Yellow River immediate drowning range is the best-documented direct figure. The 1938 Changsha fire is often cited at about 30,000 deaths but is not included in the numeric range due to

Total deaths from the 1938 Nationalist dike breach, including drowning, famine, disease, exposure, and displacement. Specialist summaries often give 400,000-500,000; broader encyclopedic ranges reach 900,000.

Attribution is mixed: drought, locusts, Japanese occupation, Nationalist requisitioning and relief failures, market disruption, and divided control all contributed. Brutal conscription and transport deaths are well documented, but no consensus figure is used here. R. J. Rummel's estimate of 3,081,000 conscripts dead from Nationalist mistreatment is cited in casualty compilations but is controversial and not rolled into totals as a firm figure.

side: Empire of Japan and collaborationist regimes
notes: Low is the Library of Congress guide's Japanese soldiers killed on Chinese soil. Point uses a Japanese-front figure including Northeast China cited in Genocide and Mass Violence in Asia. High uses a Yomiuri-derived estimate summarized in casualty literature; scopes differ.

side: Empire of Japan and collaborationist regimes
notes: The 3.9 million low uses Rummel's China-specific estimate of direct Japanese operational killing, mostly civilians. A high value is left null because broad civilian-death totals include indirect famine, disease, forced labor, displacement, and Chinese-caused disasters.

side: Empire of Japan and collaborationist regimes
notes: Three Alls Policy / sanko sakusen. Himeta estimates roughly 2.47-2.7 million Chinese civilians killed directly and indirectly.

side: Empire of Japan and collaborationist regimes
notes: The in-facility Unit 731 death figure is much lower, about 3,000-5,000 detainees at Pingfang; wider biological-warfare estimates range from about 200,000-500,000 to a 580,000 symposium figure.

side: Empire of Japan and collaborationist regimes
notes: Datong is a documented subcase, not an all-China forced-labor total. Earlier estimates cite over 60,000 miners' lives; later research by Li Jinwen gives more than 155,000 laborers.

side: Empire of Japan and collaborationist regimes
notes: Large but not cleanly separable from the overall Chinese civilian-death totals. Westad/Mitter-style estimates imply about 12 million Chinese civilian deaths; Lary gives 20-30 million total Chinese soldiers and civilians dead.

war_name: Soviet repression: Holodomor and Great Purge
side: Soviet party-state / OGPU-NKVD / Stalinist security apparatus
notes: At least 780 general-grade Red Army officers were executed during the 1937–1938 military purge; total military personnel killed is unknown. The figure overlaps with Great Terror execution totals and should not be added separately in a grand total without deduplication.

war_name: Soviet repression: Holodomor and Great Purge
side: Soviet party-state / OGPU-NKVD / Stalinist security apparatus
notes: The best documented direct killing figure is 681,692 executions in 1937–1938. A civilian-only total cannot be precisely separated because the execution total includes some military and state personnel; older or broader estimates put people 'put to death' around one million.

war_name: Soviet repression: Holodomor and Great Purge
side: Soviet party-state / OGPU-NKVD / Stalinist security apparatus

war_name: Soviet repression: Holodomor and Great Purge
side: Soviet party-state / OGPU-NKVD / Stalinist security apparatus
notes: Best modern demographic work estimates 3.9 million direct excess deaths and 0.6 million lost births in Soviet Ukraine; broader public ranges sometimes extend to 5 million.

war_name: Soviet repression: Holodomor and Great Purge
side: Soviet party-state / OGPU-NKVD / Stalinist security apparatus
notes: Common scholarly shorthand is about 1.5 million deaths; wider summaries give 1.3–2.3 million, most victims being ethnic Kazakhs.

war_name: Soviet repression: Holodomor and Great Purge

war_name: Soviet repression: Holodomor and Great Purge

war_name: Soviet repression: Holodomor and Great Purge
side: Soviet party-state / OGPU-NKVD / Stalinist security apparatus

USHMM gives 250,000–500,000 Roma murdered; other museum summaries state over 250,000.

Includes direct civilian killing by Allied bombing and Soviet security/military violence. Does not include ordinary battlefield collateral deaths where perpetrator cannot be reliably identified.

notes: Within Holocaust/Nazi-genocide accounting, the targeted groups were overwhelmingly victims, not perpetrators of systematic civilian mass killing.

war_name: World War II - Pacific Theater / Asia-Pacific War
notes: The Allied submarine campaign and mining/blockade severely disrupted food and fuel flows, but I did not find a reliable separable death toll attributable specifically to blockade-induced civilian starvation in Japan.
No famine-specific military killed category is normally counted. This excludes unrelated World War II combat deaths in the Burma campaign or Indian Army service elsewhere.
No major body of scholarship treats the Bengal famine death toll as direct killings by British or Allied forces through massacre, shooting, or bombing. Deaths were indirect famine and disease deaths.

This is the overall famine excess-mortality range under British wartime rule and policy failure, not a clean apportionment proving that every death was caused solely by one British decision. Official inquiry gave about 1.5 million; later demographic and historical work often gives about 2.1 million

The Famine Inquiry Commission recorded 66,563 boats in the denial area and reported that removal of so many boats restricted foodgrain movement and severely affected fishermen. It also recorded that more than 30,000 families were required to evacuate homes and land for military purposes, many becomi

Disease caused a large share of excess deaths, especially in 1944 after the worst food-price crisis had begun to ease. The literature does not allow a clean separation between starvation deaths and disease deaths caused by wartime policy failure.
No Japanese military deaths are counted in the Bengal famine toll. Japanese deaths in the Burma campaign are outside this famine accounting.
Japanese forces did not occupy Bengal during the famine, and the famine toll is not normally counted as direct Japanese killings inside Bengal.

Japan's conquest of Burma ended rice imports from Burma and helped trigger severe price and supply shocks in Indian rice markets. The Famine Inquiry Commission treated this as an important factor but did not provide a separate mortality estimate attributable solely to Japan.
This was not a military side; it is included as a causal actor because official and scholarly sources discuss hoarding, profiteering, and unequal access to food.
No separate direct-killing toll is attributed to private market actors. Their role was indirect through speculation, hoarding, price escalation, and unequal access to food.

The Famine Inquiry Commission wrote that enormous profits were made and that profits for some meant death for others, but it did not estimate a separate death toll attributable to traders, hoarders, or large producers. These deaths overlap with the total famine toll attributed to administrative and

Adrian Vickers' range as summarized by NIOD: 45,000-100,000 Indonesian military fatalities. Attribution by killer is not clean because deaths occurred against Dutch, British/Indian, Japanese, and rival Indonesian forces.

The high value reflects the Bersiap range only; anti-Chinese and internal-political victims may overlap with broader civilian-fatality estimates and cannot be cleanly added. Indonesian revolutionary forces were fragmented; some killings were by local militias rather than by the central Republican st
Republican and militia actions included deportations, forced evacuations, looting, and burning of property, especially during Dutch offensives. No separable indirect-death estimate was found.
Scorched-earth actions and evacuations caused hardship and displacement, but no credible separate mortality estimate was found.

Dutch military action caused displacement, village destruction, and economic disruption, but no credible separable indirect-death estimate was found.
The war produced shortages and split Dutch/Republican economies, but sources do not provide a defensible indirect mortality count attributable to Dutch policy alone.

British-caused civilian deaths occurred, especially at Surabaya, but the accessible sources do not permit a credible civilian-only count.

side: Japanese surrender forces / Japanese troops in Indonesia

notes: Japanese surrender forces fought Indonesian pemuda while trying to maintain order and guard arms. No robust total of civilians directly killed by Japanese forces in the post-surrender revolutionary period was found.

notes: The main attributed civilian-death estimate for Viet Minh direct violence is Dommen's 100,000-150,000 assassinated civilians. Attribution is contested and the figure is not independently verified by a truth commission.

side: Viet Minh / Democratic Republic of Vietnam side

side: Viet Minh / Democratic Republic of Vietnam side
notes: ANAPI reports very high mortality on forced marches and cites a claim that about 70% of Dien Bien Phu prisoners died after four months of captivity, but this is not a total-war aggregate.

The high estimate uses a broader tabulation of about 75,581 French Union dead plus 58,877 State of Vietnam dead or missing, about 134,500. Some categories may overlap, so the high estimate should be treated cautiously.

The 60,000-250,000 range is used as the main attribution band for French-caused direct civilian deaths. Individual massacre figures are illustrative and included within, not added on top of, that range.
French forces practiced torture against Viet Minh prisoners, but I found no reliable aggregate death toll for deaths caused by French detention and interrogation policies.
ANAPI reports that camps transferred in 1952-1953 to Vietnamese National Army administration were associated with summary executions, but no usable aggregate total is provided.

Britannica reports about 600,000 Nationalist troops killed in combat. Rummel gives 1,201,000 total Chinese war/rebellion dead for 1945-1949 but does not split them by side, so a side-specific high estimate is not assigned.

Only the 228 Incident is given a discrete numeric range. Rummel attributes 2,645,000 civil-war-period democide victims to the Nationalists, but that aggregate includes direct and indirect deaths and is not placed wholly in this direct-killing field.

Rummel attributes 2,645,000 democide victims to the Nationalists in the 1945-1949 civil-war period. This is an aggregate that likely includes both direct and indirect deaths; it cannot be cleanly split into direct executions versus indirect policy deaths.

side: Chinese Communist Party / People's Liberation Army

side: Chinese Communist Party / People's Liberation Army
notes: No clean direct-civilian-killing subtotal was found. Rummel's Communist democide figure is treated as an aggregate in deaths_from_actions rather than assigned entirely to direct killing.

side: Chinese Communist Party / People's Liberation Army

side: Chinese Communist Party / People's Liberation Army
notes: Rummel attributes 2,323,000 democide victims to the Communists in the 1945-1949 civil-war period. This is an aggregate including direct and indirect deaths and overlaps with specific events such as Changchun if those are included in the underlying democide estimate.
No reliable aggregate found for combatant deaths among Muslim/Pakistan-side irregulars, cadres, police deserters, or militias. Partition violence was primarily communal and civilian.

Victim-side estimates are used because the literature rarely provides perpetrator-side totals. Muslim/Pakistan-side actors directly killed large numbers of Hindu and Sikh civilians, but a clean aggregate is not available.
Indirect deaths from forced flight, disease, exposure, and hunger are not separated from direct massacre deaths in the available sources.

Victim-side estimates are used because the literature rarely provides perpetrator-side totals. Hindu-Sikh/India-side actors directly killed large numbers of Muslim civilians, but a clean aggregate is not available.
Indirect deaths from forced flight, disease, exposure, hunger, and refugee movement are not separated from direct massacre deaths in the available sources.

side: British colonial, successor-dominion, and princely-state authorities
notes: The rushed partition, late boundary announcement, inadequate security, and refugee-protection failures were important causal conditions, but no reliable numerical share of deaths can be assigned to this bucket.

Computed as 3,700-7,000 Arab-state soldiers plus 1,953 Palestinians listed by al-Aref as having died in combat situations. The Palestinian combatant figure is a known/listed subset and may undercount irregulars.

South Korean conscripts died from starvation, disease, and exposure after corruption and embezzlement in the National Defense Corps supply system; some sources give an upper figure as high as 120,000, but 50,000-90,000 is the more commonly cited range.

North Korea's invasion and later Chinese intervention contributed to mass displacement and indirect mortality, but available sources do not provide a defensible DPRK/PRC-specific indirect-death subtotal separable from the overall civilian death toll.

notes: OAS casualties caused are treated separately from the French state because the OAS also fought the French government and sought to sabotage the peace settlement.

side: MNA and other Algerian nationalist rivals
notes: No clean, widely accepted military death toll for the MNA as a separate belligerent was found. Many deaths are folded into FLN-MNA internecine violence or broader Algerian totals.

side: MNA and other Algerian nationalist rivals
notes: These deaths overlap with the FLN-MNA café-war entry above and should not be double-counted in totals.

side: People's Republic of China / Chinese Communist Party state apparatus
notes: Not a conventional armed conflict; no comprehensive famine-specific estimate of PLA or security-force deaths while serving was found. A related Great Leap Forward-era suppression episode, the Xunhua Incident, is reported as killing 17 PLA soldiers, but this is not an aggregate military-casualty figu

side: People's Republic of China / Chinese Communist Party state apparatus
notes: Direct civilian killing was real and widespread in archival and survivor accounts, but the national number is much less securely established than total excess mortality. The 2-3 million range is primarily associated with Dikötter and should be treated as an estimate, not a census count.

side: People's Republic of China / Chinese Communist Party state apparatus
notes: Derived non-overlap estimate: mainstream total excess deaths of about 15-45 million minus the direct-killing range of about 2-3 million. Demographic studies usually estimate total excess mortality rather than cleanly separating starvation, disease, exhaustion, overwork, direct violence, and suicide.

side: People's Republic of China / Chinese Communist Party state apparatus
notes: Dikötter's total includes people 'worked' to death, but accessible summaries do not provide a separate nationwide number for forced-labor or overwork deaths alone.

war_name: Vietnam War (Second Indochina War, incl. Cambodia/Laos bombing)
notes: Known/common estimates: South Vietnam 200,000-250,000; United States 58,220; South Korea about 5,099; Australia about 523-526; Thailand about 351; New Zealand 37; Philippines 9; Taiwan about 25. Khmer Republic and Royal Lao losses are not reliably separable here.

war_name: Vietnam War (Second Indochina War, incl. Cambodia/Laos bombing)
notes: The low/high are rough, non-additive attribution bounds. South Vietnam, Cambodia, and North Vietnam bombing/firepower categories overlap with broader civilian death estimates; Laos is not included numerically because killed-versus-wounded is not separated.

war_name: Vietnam War (Second Indochina War, incl. Cambodia/Laos bombing)
notes: The Senate Refugee Subcommittee recorded 11,683,000 cumulative refugee displacements in South Vietnam since 1964-1965, but deaths from disease, exposure, hunger, and collapse of care are not separately counted.

war_name: Vietnam War (Second Indochina War, incl. Cambodia/Laos bombing)
notes: The U.S. VA states more than 19 million gallons of tactical herbicides were sprayed from 1962-1971. Vietnam reports 400,000 deaths or permanent injuries and 500,000 birth defects, but no clean death-only count is established; not rolled into totals.

war_name: Vietnam War (Second Indochina War, incl. Cambodia/Laos bombing)
notes: Vietnam's later official survey is reported as 849,018 confirmed PAVN/VC military deaths for roughly 1960-1975; the 1995 official figure was about 1.1 million North Vietnamese and Viet Cong fighters killed. Chinese, Soviet, and North Korean losses were much smaller and are often reported separately;

war_name: Vietnam War (Second Indochina War, incl. Cambodia/Laos bombing)
notes: Low/high are rough and non-additive. The low combines Lewy's Vietnam assassination figure with Sliwinski-derived Cambodian assassinations; the high combines Rummel's high PAVN/VC democide figure with Cambodian assassination deaths. Some battlefield civilian deaths from communist offensives are not a

war_name: Vietnam War (Second Indochina War, incl. Cambodia/Laos bombing)
notes: Communist offensives, coercive village control, shelling, mined roads, and siege conditions contributed to large refugee flows and indirect mortality, but deaths from disease, hunger, and exposure are not separately quantified in the sources reviewed.

side: Congolese central government and pro-government forces
notes: Documented minimum from PRIO/Clodfelter for the 1964 anti-Simba campaign: about 200 Congolese soldiers and 35 mercenaries killed. Total government-side losses across all phases are not cleanly reported.

side: Congolese central government and pro-government forces
notes: The direct-civilian low combines the best documented South Kasai and Kikwit minimums. A much higher figure is possible, but higher Kwilu repression figures conflict with other sub-conflict totals.

side: Congolese central government and pro-government forces
notes: Displacement after ethnic conflict and the ANC offensive contributed to famine and epidemic malnutrition; sources report at least 200 refugees dying per day in December 1960, but no reliable cumulative total.

side: Congolese central government and pro-government forces
notes: ANC and mercenary counterinsurgency reportedly razed villages and destroyed harvests; indirect deaths from hunger and disease are not separable from direct killings in available sources.

side: Katanga and South Kasai secessionist forces
notes: Documented Katangese/secessionist losses include 202 killed in Operation Unokat and 50+ in Operation Grandslam. PRIO/Clodfelter gives 503 battle deaths in Katanga in 1961 and 80 in 1962, but those are conflict totals rather than clean Katanga-only losses.

side: Katanga and South Kasai secessionist forces
notes: Only the Lumumba/Mpolo/Okito execution is a cleanly attributable minimum. Larger Baluba civilian deaths are known but not assignable solely to secessionist forces.

side: Katanga and South Kasai secessionist forces
notes: Katangese/local violence contributed to Baluba displacement and famine; sources report 35,000 refugees in Elisabethville camps and as many as 100,000 seeking refuge in Bakwanga, but no secessionist-only indirect death total.

side: Lumumbist / CNL / Simba and Kwilu rebels
notes: PRIO/Clodfelter reports 2,000 or more Simba killed in action and another 2,000 executed. Kwilu and other rebel military deaths are not cleanly tabulated.

side: Lumumbist / CNL / Simba and Kwilu rebels
notes: Low is a conservative documented minimum from foreign-hostage and Stanleyville political killings. Larger civilian totals are partly two-sided or overlap with government repression.

side: Lumumbist / CNL / Simba and Kwilu rebels
notes: Sources describe hunger, disease, and displacement as rebel-held areas collapsed, but indirect deaths caused by rebel governance cannot be separated from the effects of government counterinsurgency.

side: United Nations Operation in the Congo (ONUC)
notes: Official UN mission page lists 245 military personnel killed/died, plus 5 international civilian staff, for 250 ONUC fatalities.

side: United Nations Operation in the Congo (ONUC)
notes: No reliable aggregate civilian death toll caused directly by ONUC fire was found.
Operation Dragon Rouge lists 2 Belgian paratroopers killed and 12 wounded. Total Belgian losses across all regular/covert/adviser categories are not cleanly reported.
No quantified civilian deaths directly caused by Belgian/U.S. intervention forces as distinct from allied Congolese/Katangese forces were found.

side: State of Guatemala and allied forces
notes: No reliable per-side total for government/PAC/security-force combatants killed was found. UCDP/PRIF reports 1,019 battle-related deaths for 1975-1995 and an adjusted UCDP-based total of about 3,461 deaths including one-sided violence and missing years, but these are not state military KIA figures an

side: State of Guatemala and allied forces
notes: The figures are responsibility-share proxies, not a forensic perpetrator-by-perpetrator death census. CEH responsibility shares are for documented violations and acts of violence, not solely deaths.

side: State of Guatemala and allied forces
notes: CEH documented destruction of homes, cattle, crops, collective fields and harvests, razing of villages, pursuit and bombing of displaced persons, and deliberate infliction of destructive living conditions. Sanford summarizes CEH as reporting 1.5 million displaced and 150,000 refugees in Mexico. No d
No reliable total of guerrilla combatants killed was found. UCDP/PRIF battle-related deaths are not disaggregated into guerrilla and government combatants.
The figures are responsibility-share proxies, not a precise death census. CEH found guerrilla abuses grave but far smaller in scale than state violence.

Best-cited side-specific figure is up to 26,000 Egyptian troops killed. This excludes unquantified Yemeni republican combatant deaths. Earlier official Egyptian army figures reported 15,194 dead, but later summaries commonly use 26,000.

The 204-224 range is only the sum of named, documented Egyptian or Egyptian-run republican gas/air-raid incidents found in accessible sources. It is not a total civilian death toll; approximately 40 chemical-weapons incidents were reportedly recorded, but no reliable aggregate death toll was found.
Egyptian bombing and poison-gas attacks have been described as part of a scorched-earth or countryside-depopulation approach, but no defensible numeric estimate of indirect deaths was found.
The war produced hunger, hardship, and dislocation; no source found separates disease or deprivation deaths caused by republican/Egyptian actions from the war's combined effects and drought.

A historical account reports an estimated 40,000 royalist dead by the mid-war stalemate period. Some summaries separately list 1,000 Saudi dead, but that figure is less well cross-checked and is not included in the numeric low here.

No credible quantified estimate was found for civilians directly killed by royalist forces. Royalist forces conducted sieges, shelling, and attacks, including the Seventy Day Siege of Sanaa, but civilian fatalities are not separated from combatant deaths in accessible sources.

Royalist siege warfare likely contributed to hunger and deprivation, especially during the Seventy Day Siege of Sanaa, but no reliable numeric allocation of indirect civilian deaths to royalist actions was found.
The war and a long drought together caused hunger, economic hardship, social dislocation, and many deaths, but sources do not allow side-specific quantification.

Known security-force deaths inflicted by the G30S side include six senior Army generals in Jakarta plus other Army officers such as Pierre Tendean, Katamso and Sugiyono; the high estimate counts police guard K.S. Tubun as a security-force combatant. These are initial coup-related deaths, not convent

The direct civilian killing range is the main death toll of the conflict. A wider 500,000-3,000,000 figure appears in Indonesian public/Komnas HAM-related reporting, but the JSON roll-up uses the more defensible scholarly/human-rights range of 200,000-1,000,000.

At least 600,000 people were imprisoned according to the IPT, and official mid-1970s figures variously stated 500,000, 600,000, or 750,000 arrested/detained. Some were executed or died in captivity, but no reliable national death total separate from direct massacre deaths was found.

About 11,500-11,600 political prisoners were sent to Buru Island and subjected to forced labor/enslavement conditions. Deaths occurred in captivity, but no defensible Buru or national camp-death estimate could be established from the accessible sources.
Komnas HAM/IPT materials record roughly 32,774 enforced disappearances, but this is not equivalent to a separate death toll and likely overlaps with killings and detentions.

side: PKI / G30S-aligned or alleged leftist side
notes: Some G30S participants and PKI leaders were killed or executed during the suppression, but no reliable aggregate combatant-death figure could be found that is separable from the mass killing of civilian alleged PKI supporters.

side: PKI / G30S-aligned or alleged leftist side
notes: The initial G30S civilian deaths were politically consequential but numerically tiny compared with the subsequent anti-leftist massacres.

Israeli Ministry of Defense pages give 736 soldiers killed in the war on one timeline and 776 dead between 5 and 14 June 1967 on the 'Price of War' page. Some secondary tables give an upper figure of 983, but 776 is the best-supported official working figure.

No reliable war-wide count of Arab civilians killed directly by Israeli forces during 5-10 June 1967 was found. Documented non-belligerent deaths caused by Israel include 15 UNEF peacekeepers and 34 USS Liberty personnel, but these are not counted as Arab civilian deaths.

UNRWA reported over 300,000 persons, including about 120,000 registered refugees, rendered homeless or leaving their homes; other summaries give roughly 280,000-325,000 Palestinians displaced. No reliable death toll from this displacement was found.

UNRWA reported Syrian movement of the order of 80,000, while other summaries give about 100,000 displaced Syrians. Akevot cites ICRC material and an Israeli census showing only 6,396 Syrian residents remaining in the occupied Golan in August 1967. No reliable death toll from this displacement was fo

Roll-up: Egypt 9,800-15,000 killed or missing; Jordan 696-700 killed; Syria 1,000-2,500 killed; Iraq 1-10 killed. Broader summaries often give 15,000-20,000 Arab soldiers killed, or more than 15,000 Arab fatalities.

The clearest civilian-death count attributable to Arab coalition action is 20 Israeli civilians killed. No comparable, reliable total was found for civilians killed by Arab forces outside Israel during the six-day combat phase.

war_name: Nigerian Civil War (Biafra) and Biafran famine
notes: No reliable Federal-only military-death split found. Commonly cited combined combatant deaths for both sides are 45,000–100,000; some sources summarize about 100,000 military casualties/deaths overall.

war_name: Nigerian Civil War (Biafra) and Biafran famine
notes: The low estimate is only a documented floor from Asaba. Other Federal direct civilian deaths from bombing, advancing troops, reprisals, and minority-area killings are real but not reliably aggregated.

war_name: Nigerian Civil War (Biafra) and Biafran famine
notes: Starvation and disease deaths caused principally by the Federal blockade, encirclement, food cutoff, and disruption of agriculture. Some broader war-death estimates go to 3,000,000 overall, but 500,000–2,000,000 is used here for the famine/deprivation bucket.

war_name: Nigerian Civil War (Biafra) and Biafran famine
notes: Disease mortality is intertwined with starvation and displacement in the famine estimates and cannot be reliably separated from the blockade/famine bucket.

war_name: Nigerian Civil War (Biafra) and Biafran famine
notes: No reliable Biafra-only military-death total found. Commonly cited combined combatant deaths for both sides are 45,000–100,000.

war_name: Nigerian Civil War (Biafra) and Biafran famine
notes: Biafran direct civilian killings are attested in several incident reports, especially against minorities accused of sabotage, but no reliable aggregate low/high estimate was found.

war_name: Nigerian Civil War (Biafra) and Biafran famine
notes: The Federal blockade was the primary famine driver, but Biafran decisions also mattered. Sources argue that relief aid strengthened Biafra and likely lengthened the war by more than a year, and that Ojukwu resisted some daylight relief flights and mercy-corridor proposals partly to preserve arms-sup

Covers Pakistani armed-forces deaths in the 1971 war; collaborator militia deaths are not reliably separated. Lower figure is a commonly cited estimate; higher figure appears in military-casualty summaries for Army, Navy and Air Force.

The range is extremely contested. The low end is the Pakistan GHQ/Hamoodur Rahman figure; the high end is Bangladesh's official 3 million figure. Scholarly estimates often avoid a clean direct/indirect split.

Estimated excess deaths among roughly 9.9 million refugees in Indian camps due to elevated mortality from disease, malnutrition, overcrowding and poor camp conditions caused by flight from the war; 95% confidence interval 323,562–802,268.

Gerlach argues rural dwellers were heavily affected by conflict-related famine and that 16–17 million people were internally displaced, but the accessible summary does not provide a separable death total for this category.

Low adds 3,713 Bangladeshi armed-forces dead from Bangladesh defence-ministry statistics to a low estimate of 2,500 Indian military dead. High adds a broader Mukti Bahini/Bangladesh Liberation Army estimate of about 30,000 killed to 3,843 Indian military dead. Categories overlap and are not standard
Bihari/non-Bengali deaths are among the most disputed parts of the war's casualty record. The best usable range found here is 1,000–150,000, with no reliable point estimate.

Israeli military deaths are relatively well documented but vary by source and inclusion criteria. Israel State Archives uses about 2,656 soldiers killed; other summaries give a 2,521–2,800 range; an Agranat-related summary gives 2,569; JVL/OnWar gives 2,688.
The low count includes Damascus and Latakia civilian deaths that could be specifically sourced. The high count adds the Egyptian claim of 200 killed in Port Said.

side: Arab coalition led by Egypt and Syria
notes: Arab military deaths are disputed. A common range gives 8,000 low and 18,500 high; other figures include 7,700 Egyptian and 3,500 Syrian dead, 15,000 Egyptian and 3,500 Syrian dead, and an Agranat-related summary of 10,000 Egyptian and 4,000 Syrian dead. Smaller-contingent casualty estimates, especi

side: Arab coalition led by Egypt and Syria
notes: No Israeli civilian deaths were found in the casualty summaries consulted. Alleged POW executions are counted as combatant/atrocity issues, not civilian deaths.

HRW/Africa states that at least 100,000 soldiers were killed in the wars, but does not fully split those deaths by side. Most large battlefield losses described in the sources were Ethiopian government soldiers/conscripts; the figure is used as a conservative all-sides military floor, not a precise

The 150,000 figure is the best conservative floor for direct civilian killing by the Derg state. Named incidents do not sum to the total because many massacres and bombings were never fully documented.
HRW/Africa estimated total famine deaths in 1983-85 at 400,000-590,000 and attributed 225,000-317,000 to government human-rights violations and counterinsurgency policies.
Evil Days gives at least 50,000 deaths from resettlement; Reckoning Under the Law says about 600,000 were forcibly relocated and perhaps 100,000 died.
HRW identified military requisitioning and blockade as creating severe hunger in Asmara in 1990-91, but no defensible separate mortality estimate was found.

PRIF/UCDP gives 98,927 fatalities from fighting between Eritrean rebels and the Ethiopian government, and cites another estimate of 200,000 for the Eritrean secession war, but these are all-sides/all-persons dyad totals and cannot be converted into EPLF combatant deaths.
EPLF abuses were documented, but available sources do not support a responsible aggregate civilian-death total.
PRIF/UCDP gives 53,483 deaths for the TPLF/EPDM/EPRDF vs government dyad, including 484 one-sided government-violence victims, but does not split deaths by side or combatant/civilian status.
HRW documents abuses but states that TPLF/EPRDF abuses were on a much smaller scale than government abuses. No comprehensive direct-civilian-death total was found.

side: OLF and other non-EPLF / non-EPRDF insurgents
notes: PRIF/UCDP gives 1,484 deaths for the OLF-government war, including 364 one-sided government-violence victims, but does not provide OLF combatant deaths. Other groups are even less well quantified.

side: OLF and other non-EPLF / non-EPRDF insurgents
notes: Real abuses are documented, but the available sources do not permit a responsible aggregate death toll.
No reliable aggregate by side found. ICTJ notes an estimated 800 Christian-militia fighters killed in the Tel al-Zaatar attack, but this is event-specific, not a war total.
Low/high are event-based documented minima using only major identified events and excluding many smaller killings and shelling deaths.
Many died from shortage of water and medication, but these deaths are embedded in the 2,200-4,280 Tel al-Zaatar total.
ICTJ records expulsions such as 150 civilians from Haret al-Ghawarneh-Antelias and 450 from Sebnay, but no separate death toll.

No reliable 1976-1990 total. Event-specific estimates include about 1,000-1,200 Syrian soldiers killed in the 1982 war.
No safe aggregate; many Syrian-caused casualties are mixed with allied-proxy actions or reciprocal shelling.
ICTJ notes deaths from lack of medicine and healthcare during the 1989 siege/bombardment, but no separate indirect total.
Hundreds arrested by Syrian military intelligence in 1989 and many transferred to Syria in 1990; deaths in custody not comprehensively quantified for 1975-1990.

side: Israel / IDF and South Lebanon Army
notes: Range reflects narrower Israeli war accounting versus broader 1982-1985 killed-in-action figure cited by the Center for Israel Education. SLA losses are not included reliably.

side: Israel / IDF and South Lebanon Army
notes: Israel cut water and electricity to at least 300,000 civilians for about three months; no separate indirect-death total found.

side: Lebanese state forces / Lebanese Army factions
notes: No reliable aggregate. ICTJ reports 30-73 captured Lebanese Army soldiers executed by Syrian forces in October 1990, but this is event-specific.

notes: Army factions changed allegiance and operated separately; no reliable aggregate civilian toll by Lebanese state forces found.

notes: No reliable aggregate. ICTJ records some event-specific fighter losses, such as Amal reporting 53 fighters killed and Hezbollah 30 in one 1988 episode.
