notes: War of the Camps deaths include civilians and fighters; no complete civilian-only aggregate exists.

side: Amal Movement and allied Shi'a/Lebanese units
notes: Amal blockaded camps during the War of the Camps, but starvation/medical deprivation deaths are not separately enumerated.

side: Hezbollah / Islamic Jihad Organization and related pro-Iranian cells
notes: No reliable total for the side's own fighters. Attacks attributed to Islamic Jihad/pro-Iranian cells caused major foreign military deaths, including 241 U.S. Marines and 58 French paratroopers in 1983.

side: Hezbollah / Islamic Jihad Organization and related pro-Iranian cells
notes: Attribution to Hezbollah versus Islamic Jihad and other fronts is disputed for some attacks.
October 23, 1983 barracks bombings killed 241 U.S. Marines and 58 French paratroopers; other smaller losses not comprehensively aggregated.
U.S. and French forces conducted strikes in 1983-1984, but no reliable civilian-death total attributable to MNF fire was found.

This low figure counts only identifiable foreign allied losses: at least about 2,000 Cuban troops in one PRIO-cited survey plus 54 Soviet deaths from a compiled casualty table. Other sources give 4,000 Cuban deaths or 2,016-5,000 Cuban deaths. Angolan government military losses are not cleanly separ
At least several thousand direct civilian deaths are attributable to government forces, but no comprehensive perpetrator-coded total was found.

MSF documented government forced displacement and regroupment policies from 1998-2002, including death-site regroupment areas. Mortality surveys recorded extreme rates, but deaths cannot be cleanly apportioned between government actions, UNITA actions, battle, disease, and blocked aid.
MSF describes systematic scorched-earth and population-control practices intended to deprive UNITA of resources; no all-war death total is available.

MSF recorded crude mortality rates such as 5.5 per 10,000 people per day in Bunjei and 3.7 per 10,000 per day in another Bunjei survey period, with deaths from malnutrition, malaria, diarrhea, respiratory infections, and measles. These are local survey findings, not a national perpetrator-coded tota

This low figure counts only identifiable foreign allied losses: PRIO cites roughly 800-1,000 South African personnel killed and a few hundred Zairian dead in some surveys. UNITA/FNLA combatant losses are not available as a reliable all-war total.
The low figure is the sum of named HRW/MSF deadly contexts only and should not be treated as an all-war total for UNITA direct civilian killing.
HRW attributed 20,000-30,000 deaths to UNITA's siege of Kuito; this overlaps with direct shelling and starvation mechanisms and should not be double-counted with the direct-civilian bucket.
HRW attributed about 10,000 deaths to UNITA's siege of Huambo; this overlaps with direct shelling and starvation mechanisms and should not be double-counted with the direct-civilian bucket.
MSF documented UNITA forcing civilians to move with its retreating forces, preventing civilians from leaving, and using civilians as logistical support; no national death total was found.

UNITA blocked freedom of movement and relief access in some areas; MSF and HRW link these practices to famine and high mortality, but attribution overlaps with government offensives and broader collapse.
UCDP/PRIO codes Cabinda/FLEC separately in selected years, but the available summarized estimates do not provide reliable all-period FLEC military deaths.
Information is described by PRIO as extremely limited. Reports mention abuses by both FAA and FLEC, but no reliable all-period civilian-death total attributable to FLEC was found.

Gerry van Klinken's corrected analysis of an Indonesian official list gives about 3,600 combat deaths, including about 2,100 mostly Indonesian regulars/police and about 40% East Timorese irregulars. This is combat deaths, not all-cause military deaths, and excludes unregistered civilians forced to a

CAVR documented-case tables attribute 67.6% of reported unlawful killings and 86.3% of enforced disappearances to Indonesian military/police and Timorese auxiliaries. The nationalized number here is a proportional calculation, not a separate CAVR perpetrator-specific estimate.

HRDAG's conservative statistical estimate is 84,200 excess hunger/illness deaths with a 95% margin of error of +/- 12,000; HRDAG's upper-bound model is 183,000. CAVR's responsibility chapter finds the Government of Indonesia and Indonesian security forces primarily responsible for 100,000-180,000 hu

Deaths are included within the excess hunger/illness bucket. HRDAG estimated 108,200 households experienced 282,800 displacement events, and CAVR tied displacement, mortality, resettlement camps, destruction of food sources, and denial of aid to Indonesian occupation policy.
No reliable total Falintil/Fretilin combatant-death estimate was found. CAVR/HRDAG explicitly did not estimate combatant deaths.

CAVR indicates many Fretilin-attributed killings occurred in the 1975 internal party conflict. These are documented-case proportions applied to a national estimate, not a separate CAVR statistical perpetrator estimate.

HRDAG reports Falintil was named as the institution telling households to move in 15.0% of displacement reports, but no reliable source provides a separate death toll from Falintil-caused displacement. Most famine/deprivation deaths are attributed by CAVR to Indonesian invasion and occupation polici

side: Other East Timorese political factions and non-TNI-attributed actors
notes: No reliable all-cause combatant-death total found for these factions.

side: Other East Timorese political factions and non-TNI-attributed actors
notes: These estimates should be treated cautiously because CAVR did not publish an independent national statistical estimate by each minor perpetrator group.

side: Democratic Kampuchea / Khmer Rouge / Communist Party of Kampuchea
notes: No reliable estimate found that separates Khmer Rouge soldiers killed in combat from cadres and soldiers executed in internal purges or from post-1979 guerrilla-war losses. ECCC materials show purges of Party, army, and civilians, but do not provide a national military-only death toll.

side: Democratic Kampuchea / Khmer Rouge / Communist Party of Kampuchea
notes: Direct-killing totals are not a complete census; they synthesize survivor-survey cause shares and mass-grave mapping. Subgroup/event entries overlap with the national direct-killing estimate and should not be summed.

side: Democratic Kampuchea / Khmer Rouge / Communist Party of Kampuchea
notes: Starvation deaths are not cleanly separable nationally from disease, overwork, displacement, and direct executions. They are included in the combined indirect-deaths estimate below.

side: Democratic Kampuchea / Khmer Rouge / Communist Party of Kampuchea
notes: Forced labor and overwork deaths are documented by ECCC but not quantified nationally as a distinct category.

side: Democratic Kampuchea / Khmer Rouge / Communist Party of Kampuchea
notes: Disease deaths are not cleanly separable nationally from starvation and overwork. Sliwinski's summarized estimates assign 13% of deaths to disease, but the report uses a combined indirect bucket to avoid false precision.

side: Democratic Kampuchea / Khmer Rouge / Communist Party of Kampuchea
notes: Forced displacement contributed to death through exhaustion, starvation, disease, family separation, and subsequent execution, but is not separately quantified nationally.

side: Democratic Kampuchea / Khmer Rouge / Communist Party of Kampuchea
notes: Coherent residual estimate: total Cambodian genocide deaths of 1.2-2.8 million minus direct executions of about 0.5-1.3 million. This is a derived range, not an independent register.

side: Vietnam and Kampuchean United Front for National Salvation
notes: Vietnamese military deaths in the adjacent border war/invasion context, not part of the standard Cambodian genocide civilian toll. A Vietnamese general reported 30,000 Vietnamese soldiers killed from 1977 to 1979; a Clodfelter/OnWar-derived summary gives 28,000 Vietnam fatalities for the broader Cam

side: Vietnam and Kampuchean United Front for National Salvation
notes: No reliable genocide-period estimate found for Cambodian civilians directly killed by Vietnamese or KNUFNS forces during the overthrow of Democratic Kampuchea. Such deaths, if any, are not a major quantified category in standard Cambodian genocide casualty accounting.

side: FRELIMO-led Government of Mozambique and allied forces
notes: No authoritative per-side combatant death rollup was found. UCDP/PRIF records 13,924 total conflict deaths but does not separate FRELIMO/government, allied, RENAMO, and civilian deaths.

side: FRELIMO-led Government of Mozambique and allied forces
notes: Government abuses are documented qualitatively, but not quantified in a reliable war-wide death estimate.

side: FRELIMO-led Government of Mozambique and allied forces

side: FRELIMO-led Government of Mozambique and allied forces
notes: The broader war displaced millions and produced hunger and disease mortality; no clean FRELIMO-only allocation was found.
No authoritative RENAMO combatant death total was found. UCDP/PRIF provides a total conflict-death figure but not a per-side combatant count.
RENAMO is the only side for which the sources found provide a broad numerical estimate for direct civilian killing. The 100,000 figure should be treated as an estimate/minimum rather than a maximum.

RENAMO attacks on transport, clinics, schools, food systems, civilians, and relief networks were a major driver of famine and disease mortality, but the number attributable solely to RENAMO cannot be separated from government actions, drought, and the wider conflict. These deaths are included in the
RENAMO violence and territorial control drove mass displacement and indirect mortality; no side-specific death estimate was found.
Human Rights Watch summarizes the Gersony report as accusing RENAMO of running what were effectively slave-labor camps, but no deaths-only number for this subcategory was found.

Low combines 25,300 Vietnamese state deaths and 15,000 Cambodian state deaths in COW/Clodfelter-style accounting. High combines Vietnam's 1988 claim of 55,000 Vietnamese soldiers killed with a Cambodian government/UCDP note of over 10,000 government troops killed in combat and 20,000 deaths from dis
Civilian deaths caused by Vietnamese/PRK operations are documented, but the literature does not provide a reliable war-wide perpetrator-attributed total.

HRW reports K5 caused many thousands of deaths from disease and landmines. It also notes that if official figures were accurate, a 5-10% death rate among early workers would suggest about 1,000 malaria deaths. A Cambodia Daily summary of Gottesman reports a conservative calculation of 25,000 deaths

No reliable killed-only aggregate found for Khmer Rouge/CGDK combatants. PRIO documentation cites Khieu Samphan's claim that Khmer Rouge forces lost 30,000 killed or wounded from December 1978 through May 1979 and 2,000 from June 1979 through February 1980, but these are killed-and-wounded figures,

side: Soviet Union and DRA/Republic of Afghanistan government

side: Soviet Union and DRA/Republic of Afghanistan government

Mujahideen abuses were real and documented, but no reliable national civilian-death total attributable to them exists for the Soviet-war period.

HRW reported that mujahideen forces laid mines without adequate marking or mapping. Civilian mine deaths attributable specifically to mujahideen for 1979-1989 cannot be separated from mine casualties caused by Soviet/DRA and other actors.

Iranian missile and artillery attacks killed Iraqi civilians during the War of the Cities, but I found no reliable war-wide Iraqi civilian total attributable specifically to Iranian fire. Later Iraqi mortality work usually cannot assign responsible party.

The Iraqi military/civilian split is uncertain. PLOS cites 105,000 Iraqi deaths as a conservative estimate and Rob Johnson's 200,000 Iraqi deaths; a common military-history midpoint is about 150,000 Iraqi dead. PLOS's own estimate of 174,289 adult Iraqi direct war-injury deaths includes adult civili

side: Government of El Salvador and allied forces
notes: Government counterinsurgency tactics produced mass displacement and refugee flight, but no reliable separately quantified indirect-death estimate was found. Violent deaths from massacres, executions, shelling and bombing are counted under civilians_killed_directly.
Seligson and McElhinny are reported as estimating 12,274–23,840 FMLN combatant deaths; another common summary gives 12,274–20,000.
The FMLN was responsible for a much smaller but real share of documented abuses. The range is a proportional inference from complaint attribution and the modern civilian-death estimate.

side: Government of Sudan and aligned forces
notes: No reliable source found disaggregates Government of Sudan military deaths. notes: The overall direct civilian death toll caused by government and aligned forces is unknown. Available sources document widespread killing, bombardment, scorched earth, abduction, and militia raids but do not produce a comprehensive perpetrator-specific total.

notes: Government and allied militia actions were central to famine-producing mechanisms, including raiding, asset-stripping, displacement, and relief obstruction. HRW says the 1998 famine affected 2.6 million people and that government/militia and opposition abuses caused or worsened it, but it does not a

The UN Panel found that forced labour blurred the combatant/civilian distinction and exposed civilians to additional harm from shelling; no separate death estimate is available.

HRW reported up to 500 civilian mine casualties during the first year of the war; this is casualties rather than deaths, and both sides used mines, so no death estimate is assigned.

Low combines 5,856 Armenian military deaths in 1988-1994, 3,712 Armenian servicemen in 2020, and inferred 198 combatants/security personnel in 2023. High uses 6,000 for 1988-1994 and 3,825 for 2020, plus inferred 198 for 2023. Excludes many 1994-2019 ceasefire deaths.

HRW and ICRC documented acute shortages and disruption of food, medicine, fuel, and humanitarian movement. No audited blockade-specific mortality count was found.

Armenia's Investigative Committee said over 70 people died en route from Nagorno-Karabakh to Armenia or shortly after arrival, mostly due to severe psychological impact; this is an associated exodus-death count, not a direct-combat figure.

At least 218 died in the 25 September 2023 fuel-depot explosion while civilians were trying to obtain fuel during the mass flight. Attribution to Azerbaijani policy/action is indirect and contested; the event is best treated as exodus-associated mortality.
